topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt like sandpaper against my fingertips. I traced the elegant script, the carefully chosen anecdotes, the precise timeline of my life—all of it, meticulously constructed, and none of it was mine. A cold, nauseating wave washed over me, making my breath catch in my throat. I reread the section detailing my ‘defining moments,’ the descriptions of my triumphs, and the realization hit me with the force of a physical blow. Someone had authored my existence. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, the ink blurring beneath my anxious gaze. I snatched the document, crumpling it into a tight, furious ball, the sound echoing too loudly in the silent apartment. I paced the room, my movements jerky and frantic, searching for an escape route that didn't exist. Every shadow seemed to hold a judging eye. I needed answers, and the silence of the room was amplifying the sheer wrongness of everything.

[story 2]

My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo in the quiet library. I stared at the manuscript spread across the mahogany table—my life story, complete and damning. The words spoke of my aspirations, my supposed struggles, my very essence, yet they felt alien, like a costume I’d never worn. A sudden, sharp intake of air stole my ability to speak. I pushed my chair back so violently it scraped across the floor, the noise drawing a nearby librarian’s sharp glance. My palms were slick with sweat, and I had to grip the edge of the table to keep from shaking apart. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the frantic urge to claw at something, anything, to pull the truth out of the binding. The air felt too thin, suffocating, and the sheer weight of this fabricated narrative pressed down on my chest, making every shallow breath a conscious, painful effort.

[story 3]

I sat on the cold concrete steps, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. The document lay crumpled in my lap, the paper damp with the sudden sheen of my perspiration. Each line was a betrayal, a deliberate misdirection woven into the fabric of who I thought I was. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound of pure, contained distress. I stood up abruptly, my legs unsteady, and began to walk, not toward home, but into the anonymous flow of traffic. My eyes darted everywhere—at the hurried pedestrians, the neon signs flashing promises of escape. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I needed to dismantle this lie, to tear the scaffolding down, but the knowledge that the blueprint was already in circulation left me paralyzed, a statue carved from sheer, agonizing uncertainty.
